nativeRange.habitat: Current-swept outer reef slopes and channels; forms large aggregations in midwater feeding on zooplankton, retreating into rubble patches, crevices and caves when threatened. Juveniles shelter close to the substrate.

diet.notes: In the wild, primarily a midwater planktivore feeding on zooplankton, supplemented with sponges. In captivity it readily accepts a varied meaty diet: krill, mysis, squid, clams on the half shell, chopped shrimp, small pieces of fish, and marine pellets. Hard-shelled foods such as whole shrimp or clams are generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing red incisor-like teeth. LiveAquaria classifies it as carnivorous in captive context.

summary: A striking purple-to-blue planktivorous triggerfish with red incisor-like teeth and long trailing tail lobes. Often considered one of the more peaceful Balistidae and tentatively reef-safe with caution.
careNotes:
The Niger Trigger (also widely sold as Red-Tooth Trigger or Redtooth Triggerfish, occasionally as Blue-Throat Niger) is commonly considered one of the more peaceful and well-mannered triggerfishes for the home aquarium. Coloration varies day-to-day across purple, blue and blue-green, with lighter blue highlights along the fins and long trailing caudal lobes; mature adults develop the distinctive bright red teeth that give the species its common name.

Adults have been reported at around 30 cm in the trade, but FishBase lists a wild maximum of 50 cm TL; tank specimens commonly reach the 30-40 cm range over a long lifespan. It is generally advised to provide a tank of at least 700 L (around 180-200 US gal) with a long footprint, plentiful live rock arranged into caves and overhangs, and substantial open swimming space. Because the species is a current-swept midwater planktivore in the wild, vigorous flow and vertical swimming room matter as much as floor area. They have been known to rearrange rockwork. A secure, well-weighted lid is generally advised - triggers are powerful and can displace loose covers.

Reef compatibility is debated. The Niger Trigger is often described as reef-safe-with-caution: it has been reported to generally leave corals alone but will readily eat ornamental shrimp, small crabs, snails and other small motile invertebrates. Individual personality varies; some specimens become more nippy or territorial as they mature, especially if underfed or kept in cramped quarters. Best kept as a single specimen with robust semi-aggressive tankmates of similar size; conspecific aggression is generally high outside very large systems.

Cross-source values were reconciled: LiveAquaria lists a 30 cm max and 72-78 F (22.2-25.6 C) aquarium range with 180 gal minimum; FishBase lists a 50 cm max and 24.9-29.1 C wild range with depth to 110 m; Reef Builders and other hobby sources confirm the unusually peaceful temperament for a triggerfish. Adult size is reported as 30-50 cm; temperature averaged to 23-27 C; minimum tank biased to 700 L given the species' active midwater habit and likely 50 cm adult size.
breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. In the wild the species is oviparous with distinct pair-bonding; eggs are laid in benthic nests and both parents have been reported to guard the site, though only the female tends the eggs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care reference. Provides species ID, family, captive water parameters (72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), 180 gal minimum, aggressive temperament, not reef safe (LiveAquaria's conservative classification), carnivorous diet, easy care level.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Balistidae / Tetraodontiformes), Indo-Pacific distribution, wild depth range (5-110 m), wild temperature range (24.9-29.1 C, mean 27.9), maximum length (50 cm TL, common 30 cm), feeding biology (zooplankton + sponges), and oviparous-with-pairing reproduction. IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Reef Builders
additional.notes: Cross-check on temperament - characterizes the species as usually peaceful and well-mannered by triggerfish standards, with individual personality variation. Supports the semi-aggressive (not aggressive) classification used here.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics - Triggerfish care guide
additional.notes: Cross-check on reef-safe-with-caution status: Niger Triggers are usually coral-safe but will consume motile invertebrates and shrimp; underfeeding and cramped tanks increase aggression.

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity. Wild spawning is oviparous in benthic nests with pair-bonding and parental guarding.
